core_claim: RoguePlanet is a Windows LPE that races Microsoft Defender's quarantine pipeline: an oplock pauses a scan, a junction swap makes Defender write a SYSTEM-owned file into a user-controlled folder, and the Windows Error Reporting scheduled task then runs the planted binary as SYSTEM. No CVE, no patch; Windows 11 Pro confirmed.

slides.on_slide_copy: DEFENDER QUARANTINED THE FILE. THE QUARANTINE WAS THE EXPLOIT.
slides.subline: RoguePlanet turns a standard user into SYSTEM on a fully updated Windows 11. No CVE. No patch.

slides.subline: Quarantine is a privileged write. The gap is between creating that file and checking where its path actually points.

chain.title: Dual-mode entry
chain.detail: A named pipe (RoguePlanet) is created to spawn the final shell.

chain.title: I/O saturation
chain.detail: Poseidon threads flood the disk so the oplock race fires reliably.

chain.title: Filesystem prep
chain.detail: Mount an embedded ISO and build a fake System32 under a temp UUID.

chain.title: EICAR + oplock
chain.detail: An alternate data stream triggers a scan; an oplock freezes Defender mid-operation.

chain.title: Junction swap
chain.detail: Defender resumes and quarantines a SYSTEM-owned file into the attacker's folder.

chain.title: Payload injection
chain.detail: Overwrite that file, then junction the path to resolve as the real System32 wermgr.exe.

chain.title: WER task
chain.detail: Trigger the QueueReporting scheduled task, which a normal user can fire.

chain.title: NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM
chain.detail: SYSTEM runs the planted binary and spawns conhost.exe in the user session.

slides.on_slide_copy: There is no patch to apply and no toggle to flip. It is a design-level race between Defender, NTFS junctions, and Volume Shadow Copy.
slides.subline: Signature detection catches one compiled binary. The behavioral chain survives a recompile with minor source changes.
slides.visual_direction: cinematic, dark, single accent glow; text-free background; protected lower-third.
slides.visual_prompt: Cool light grazes a patch calendar in the dark, June dates marked green but a red zero-day marker overlapping the same week, tense editorial composition, the calendar held high with a clean lower third.

slides.on_slide_copy: Hunt the chain, not the binary: a named pipe called RoguePlanet from a non-system process, wermgr.exe running outside System32, and SYSTEM spawning conhost.exe with no command prompt in the tree.
slides.subline: Also flag Volume Shadow Copy enumeration by non-system processes and the Poseidon burst of writes to temp.
slides.visual_direction: cinematic, dark, single accent glow; text-free background; protected lower-third.

caption:
Microsoft Defender quarantined a malicious file, and that quarantine was the exploit. Meet RoguePlanet.

Nightmare Eclipse dropped it on June 10. There is no CVE and no patch, and Windows 11 Pro is confirmed vulnerable. A standard user with no admin rights ends up with a SYSTEM shell on a fully updated machine, using Defender itself as the lever.

The primitive is a race. The exploit places an opportunistic lock so Defender pauses mid-scan, then swaps an NTFS junction so Defender's quarantine writes a SYSTEM-owned file into a folder the user controls. That file gets overwritten with the payload, the path is junctioned so it resolves as the real System32 wermgr.exe, and the Windows Error Reporting QueueReporting scheduled task (which an unprivileged user can trigger) runs it as SYSTEM. The result is conhost.exe in the user's session, running as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM.

There is no toggle to fix this. It is a design-level race between Defender's scan-and-quarantine pipeline, NTFS reparse points, and the Volume Shadow Copy service. The signature only catches the compiled sample, and the behavioral chain survives a recompile. It does not work on Windows Server, because standard users there cannot mount ISOs.

So hunt the behavior: a named pipe called RoguePlanet from a non-system process, wermgr.exe running outside System32, SYSTEM spawning conhost.exe with no cmd.exe in the tree, Volume Shadow Copy enumeration by non-system processes, and a burst of writes to UUID-named temp files.

Your antivirus is not just a scanner. It is a SYSTEM-level file mover, and its quarantine can be raced.

Could your EDR catch a SYSTEM shell born from Defender's own quarantine?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[RoguePlanet, Nightmare Eclipse, Microsoft Defender, Windows zero-day, local privilege escalation, race condition, WER, endpoint detection]
comment_prompt: Could your EDR catch a SYSTEM shell that came from Defender's own quarantine?

Slide 4 (mechanism): two racing threads in the dark, one capturing the resource a microsecond before the other.
Slide 5 (chain): an on-brand step-flow diagram of the RoguePlanet attack chain, eight boxes from a named pipe down to NT AUTHORITY SYSTEM.
Slide 6 (failure point): a patch calendar, June dates marked green but a red zero-day marker overlapping the same week.
Slide 7 (defense): a split composition, left a Defender shield scanning, right the cracked shield with a SYSTEM prompt leaking out.
Slide 8 (takeaway): a shield icon, one half intact, the other half fractured with red particles escaping.
Slide 9 (cta): an event log entry on a clean dark end-card with open negative space.

sources.source: CYDERES Howler Cell: RoguePlanet Windows zero-day (technical analysis + attack-chain figure)
sources.supports: Full chain: named pipe RoguePlanet, Poseidon I/O saturation, embedded ISO mount, EICAR + :WDFOO ADS oplock pausing Defender, junction swap so Defender quarantines a SYSTEM-owned file in an attacker dir, overwrite + junction so the path resolves as C:\Windows\System32\wermgr.exe, WER QueueReporting scheduled task triggerable by an unprivileged user runs it as SYSTEM, conhost.exe in user session; root cause is a design race across Defender quarantine, NTFS reparse points, and VSS; no CVE/patch; Windows 11 Pro confirmed and Server unaffected because standard users cannot mount ISOs; signature Exploit:Win32/DfndrRugPlnt.BB catches only the compiled PoC; the listed detections.

sources.source: SecurityWeek: New Windows Zero-Day Exploit 'RoguePlanet' Released
sources.supports: Release June 10; Nightmare Eclipse / MSNightmare; LPE to SYSTEM on fully patched Windows 11; original GitHub account suspended; dropped the same week Microsoft patched GreenPlasma and YellowKey; prior Nightmare Eclipse exploits RedSun, UnDefend, BlueHammer.

sources.source: Microsoft Security Response: legal-threat clarification after disclosure backlash
sources.supports: Microsoft statement that it will not pursue security researchers; GitHub account suspension and community backlash context.

narration.text: Microsoft Defender quarantined a file, and that quarantine was the exploit. Meet RoguePlanet.
Nightmare Eclipse dropped it on June 10. No CVE, no patch, and Windows 11 Pro is confirmed vulnerable.
A standard user with no admin rights gets a SYSTEM shell on a fully updated machine, using Defender itself.
The trick is a race. An oplock freezes Defender mid-scan, then a junction swap makes it quarantine a SYSTEM-owned file into a folder you control.
Here is the whole chain. A named pipe, an I/O storm to force the race, an ISO mount, the quarantine swap, and a Windows task that runs the planted file as SYSTEM.
There is no toggle to fix it. It is a design race between Defender, NTFS junctions, and Volume Shadow Copy. The signature only catches the compiled sample.
So hunt the behavior. A pipe named RoguePlanet, the Windows error binary running outside System32, and SYSTEM spawning a console with no command prompt in the tree.
Your antivirus is not just a scanner. It is a SYSTEM-level file mover, and its quarantine can be raced.
Could your EDR catch a SYSTEM shell born from Defender's own quarantine? Tell me below.

beats.caption: June 10 drop. No CVE, no patch. Win 11 Pro.
Standard user to SYSTEM via Defender itself.
Oplock pauses the scan. Junction swaps the quarantine.
Named pipe to SYSTEM, the full chain.
No patch. A design race. Signature beats one binary.
Hunt the behavior, not the binary.
AV is a SYSTEM-level file mover that can be raced.
